title: Check path exists
description: Check if path exists between two nodes

examples.explanation: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}. Check neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1, mark visited {0,1}. Check neighbors [0,3]. Step 3: Neighbor 0 is visited, skip. Neighbor 3 is target, return True. Backtrack to step 2, return True to step 1, final output True.
Step 1: Start at 0, visited {0}, neighbors [1]. Step 2: Explore 1, visited {0,1}, neighbors [0,2]. Step 3: 0 visited, skip. 2 is target, return True. Backtrack, final True.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform a depth-first search from the start node to explore all reachable nodes, checking if the target node is found. This is a straightforward recursive or iterative traversal that exhaustively searches the graph.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute
ladders.desc: Given a graph represented as an adjacency list and two nodes, determine if there exists a path from the start node to the target node using brute force DFS.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to systematically explore all possible paths from the start node until we either find the target node or exhaust all reachable nodes. This works because DFS follows each branch as deeply as possible before backtracking, ensuring we traverse the entire connected component containing the start node. The algorithm begins by marking the start node as visited to prevent cycles. Then, recursively explore each neighbor: if the neighbor is the target, return true; otherwise, if unvisited, perform DFS from that neighbor. If all neighbors are explored without finding the target, return false. Time complexity is O(V+E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed at most once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited set and recursion stack. This feeds into the parent problem by providing a complete solution to path existence. Common mistakes include not handling cycles (leading to infinite recursion) and forgetting to mark nodes as visited before recursion. Real code pattern: def hasPath(graph, start, target, visited): if start == target: return True; visited.add(start); for neighbor in graph[start]: if neighbor not in visited and hasPath(graph, neighbor, target, visited): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 0, mark visited {0}. Check neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1, mark visited {0,1}. Check neighbors [0,3]. Step 3: Neighbor 0 is visited, skip. Neighbor 3 is target, return True. Backtrack to step 2, return True to step 1, final output True.
Step 1: Start at 0, visited {0}, neighbors [1]. Step 2: Explore 1, visited {0,1}, neighbors [0,2]. Step 3: 0 visited, skip. 2 is target, return True. Backtrack, final True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start Node
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive DFS function that explores all nodes reachable from a given start node, marking visited nodes to avoid cycles.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to traverse the graph depth-first by recursively visiting each unvisited neighbor, which ensures we cover the entire connected component. This works because recursion naturally implements the LIFO stack behavior of DFS. The algorithm: if the current node is the target, return true; mark it as visited; for each neighbor, if unvisited, recursively call DFS; if any recursive call returns true, propagate true upward; otherwise, return false. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the subgraph reachable from start, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being the core traversal mechanism. Common mistakes include not marking visited before recursion (causing infinite loops) and not handling base cases properly. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, target, visited, graph): if node == target: return True; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited and dfs(neighbor, target, visited, graph): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0 != 2, mark visited {0}. Neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1, visited {0,1}, neighbors [0]. 0 visited, skip, return False. Step 3: Explore neighbor 2, visited {0,1,2}, node 2 == target, return True. Backtrack to step 1, return True.

Step 1: Node 0 != 3, visited {0}, neighbors [1]. Step 2: Explore 1, visited {0,1}, neighbors [2]. Step 3: Explore 2, visited {0,1,2}, neighbors [], return False. Backtrack, all return False, final False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Tracking / Queue / Union
ladders.desc: Implement a mechanism to track visited nodes during graph traversal to avoid infinite recursion in cyclic graphs.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that without tracking visited nodes, DFS could revisit the same node indefinitely in cyclic graphs, causing infinite recursion or stack overflow. This works by maintaining a set (or array) of visited nodes; before exploring a node, check if it's already visited—if so, skip it. This ensures each node is processed at most once. The algorithm: initialize an empty visited set; when at a node, add it to visited; when considering neighbors, only recurse if not in visited. Time complexity is O(1) per check, but overall contributes to O(V) space. Space complexity is O(V) for the set. This feeds into L1 by enabling safe recursion. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark visited before recursion or using a local visited set per call. Real code pattern: visited = set(); def traverse(node): if node in visited: return; visited.add(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: traverse(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Node 0 not in visited, add {0}, neighbors [1]. Step 2: Neighbor 1 not in visited, recurse to node 1. Step 3: Node 1 not in visited, add {0,1}, neighbors [0]. Step 4: Neighbor 0 in visited, skip. Return, final visited {0,1}.

Step 1: Node 0 not visited, add {0}, neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Explore neighbor 1, not visited, add {0,1}, neighbors [0]. 0 visited, skip. Step 3: Explore neighbor 2, not visited, add {0,1,2}, neighbors [0]. 0 visited, skip. Final visited {0,1,2}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Given a node and its adjacency list, recursively visit each neighbor, performing an action at each node.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to use recursion to explore each neighbor one by one, which mimics the depth-first order. This works because recursion uses the call stack to remember where to backtrack. The algorithm: define a function that takes a node; perform an action (e.g., print or collect); for each neighbor in the node's adjacency list, recursively call the function. This assumes no cycles or external visited tracking. Time complexity is O(V+E) if traversing entire graph, but for this sub-problem, it's O(degree(node)) per call. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. This feeds into L2 by providing the recursive exploration mechanism. Common mistakes include infinite recursion in cyclic graphs without visited checks and not handling base cases. Real code pattern: def traverse(node): print(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: traverse(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: At node 0, print 0. Neighbors [1,2]. Step 2: Recurse to neighbor 1, print 1. Neighbors [], return. Step 3: Recurse to neighbor 2, print 2. Neighbors [], return. Final order 0,1,2.
Step 1: Node 0, print 0. Neighbors [1]. Step 2: Recurse to 1, print 1. Neighbors [2]. Step 3: Recurse to 2, print 2. Neighbors [], return. Final order 0,1,2.

testCases.expected: 0,1,0 (infinite without visited check, but for this level assume no cycles)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Access / Queue Ops
ladders.desc: Given a graph represented as an adjacency list and a node, retrieve and iterate over its list of neighboring nodes.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an adjacency list is a dictionary or array where each key/index is a node, and the value is a list of nodes it connects to. This works because it directly encodes graph edges. The algorithm: to get neighbors of node 'n', access graph[n] (e.g., in Python: graph.get(n, []) to handle missing nodes). Then, iterate over this list using a loop. Time complexity is O(1) for access and O(degree(n)) for iteration. Space complexity is O(1) extra. This feeds into L3 by providing the data needed for recursive traversal. Common mistakes include assuming graph[n] exists without checks or misunderstanding the structure. Real code pattern: neighbors = graph[node]; for neighbor in neighbors: # process neighbor.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] returns the list [1,2]. Step 2: Iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2.
Step 1: Access graph[1] returns [2]. Step 2: Iterate: neighbor 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs (nodes & edges) and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
Graphs consist of nodes (vertices) and edges (connections). We represent them using adjacency lists (dict or list of lists). 
Arrays are used to track visited nodes or parent pointers. This foundation supports all path-finding algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Add edge (0,1): graph[0] becomes [1], graph[1] becomes [0]. Step 2: Add edge (1,2): graph[1] becomes [0,2], graph[2] becomes [1]. Final graph as above.
Step 1: Add directed edge (0,1): graph[0] becomes [1], graph[1] remains [] if not initialized. Final graph as above.

testCases.input: Edges: [(0,1), (1,2)], directed from 0 to 1 and 1 to 2

approaches.name: BFS with Queue O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use a breadth-first search with a queue to explore nodes level by level from the start node, stopping if the target node is reached. This ensures the shortest path in terms of edges but focuses on existence.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of BFS to determine if a path exists between two nodes in a graph.
ladders.explanation: This is the full problem where we implement BFS from scratch to check connectivity between two nodes. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level from the start node, guaranteeing we'll find the target if it's reachable. We use a queue to manage frontier nodes and a visited set to avoid cycles. The algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with start node and mark it visited. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node, if it's target return True. 3) For each neighbor not visited: enqueue and mark visited. 4) If queue empties without finding target, return False. Time: O(V+E) where V=vertices, E=edges. Space: O(V) for queue and visited set. Connection to L1: This uses the BFS traversal subroutine from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes: forgetting to mark nodes visited when enqueuing (causing infinite loops), not handling disconnected graphs properly. Real pattern: queue = deque([start]); visited = set([start]); while queue: node = queue.popleft(); if node == target: return True; for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor); return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[0], Visited={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, not target. Neighbors 1,2 not visited. Queue=[1,2], Visited={0,1,2}. Step 3: Dequeue 1, not target. Neighbor 3 not visited. Queue=[2,3], Visited={0,1,2,3}. Step 4: Dequeue 2, not target. No new neighbors. Queue=[3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3, equals target. Return True.

Step 1: Queue=[0], Visited={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, not target. Neighbor 1 not visited. Queue=[1], Visited={0,1}. Step 3: Dequeue 1, not target. Neighbor 0 already visited. Queue empty. Step 4: Return False (target 3 never reached).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start Node
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to visit all reachable nodes from a starting node.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs BFS traversal without checking for a specific target. The intuition is to systematically explore all nodes reachable from start using a queue. Algorithm: 1) Initialize queue with start, visited set with start. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node, process it (e.g., collect in list), for each unvisited neighbor: enqueue and mark visited. 3) Return visited nodes or traversal order. Time: O(V+E). Space: O(V). Connection to L0: This is the core traversal used in L0, where L0 adds target checking. Common mistakes: processing nodes before marking visited (risk of re-enqueueing), using list instead of deque for queue (inefficient pops). Real pattern: def bfs(graph, start): visited=[]; queue=deque([start]); seen=set([start]); while queue: node=queue.popleft(); visited.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in seen: seen.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor); return visited.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[0], Seen={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, visited=[0]. Neighbors 1,2 not seen. Queue=[1,2], Seen={0,1,2}. Step 3: Dequeue 1, visited=[0,1]. Neighbor 3 not seen. Queue=[2,3], Seen={0,1,2,3}. Step 4: Dequeue 2, visited=[0,1,2]. No new neighbors. Queue=[3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3, visited=[0,1,2,3]. Queue empty. Return [0,1,2,3].
Step 1: Queue=[0], Seen={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0, visited=[0]. Neighbor 1 not seen. Queue=[1], Seen={0,1}. Step 3: Dequeue 1, visited=[0,1]. Neighbor 0 already seen. Queue empty. Return [0,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Tracking / Queue / Union
ladders.desc: Use a queue to process nodes in FIFO order, exploring neighbors level by level.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the queue mechanism for BFS. The intuition is that a queue ensures we explore nodes in the order they're discovered, which corresponds to increasing distance from start. Algorithm: 1) Enqueue start node. 2) While queue not empty: dequeue node, enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. 3) Track visited to avoid cycles. Time: O(V+E) as each node and edge processed once. Space: O(V) for queue. Connection to L1: This is the fundamental process that L1 builds upon by adding node processing. Common mistakes: using stack instead of queue (becomes DFS), not checking visited before enqueuing. Real pattern: queue=deque([start]); visited=set([start]); while queue: node=queue.popleft(); for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[0], Visited={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbors 1,2 not visited. Queue=[1,2], Visited={0,1,2}. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbor 3 not visited. Queue=[2,3], Visited={0,1,2,3}. Step 4: Dequeue 2. No new neighbors. Queue=[3]. Step 5: Dequeue 3. No new neighbors. Queue empty. Final visited={0,1,2,3}.
Step 1: Queue=[0], Visited={0}. Step 2: Dequeue 0. Neighbor 1 not visited. Queue=[1], Visited={0,1}. Step 3: Dequeue 1. Neighbor 0 already visited. Queue empty. Final visited={0,1}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Represent a graph and access neighbors of a node.
ladders.explanation: This level covers graph representation, typically as adjacency list, which is crucial for BFS. The intuition is that we need a data structure to efficiently get all neighbors of a node. Algorithm: 1) Represent graph as dictionary where keys are nodes and values are lists of neighbors. 2) For a given node, access its neighbor list. 3) Handle cases like missing nodes or empty lists. Time: O(1) to access neighbors if using hash map. Space: O(V+E) to store graph. Connection to L2: L2 uses this representation to iterate over neighbors during queue processing. Common mistakes: using adjacency matrix (wasteful for sparse graphs), not handling directed vs undirected graphs correctly. Real pattern: graph = {0:[1,2], 1:[0,3], 2:[0], 3:[1]}; neighbors_of_0 = graph[0] # returns [1,2].

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0]. Step 2: Return list [1,2] as neighbors.
Step 1: Access graph[2]. Step 2: Return list [3] as neighbors.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Access / Queue Ops
ladders.desc: Implement basic queue operations: add to back (enqueue) and remove from front (dequeue).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on queue operations, which are essential for BFS. The intuition is that a queue follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) order. Algorithm: 1) Initialize empty queue (using list or deque). 2) Enqueue: add item to end. 3) Dequeue: remove and return item from front. 4) Check if empty. Time: O(1) for enqueue/dequeue with deque. Space: O(n) for queue storage. Connection to L3: L3's graph operations feed nodes into this queue in L2. Common mistakes: using list pop(0) which is O(n), not handling empty queue on dequeue. Real pattern: from collections import deque; q = deque(); q.append(1) # enqueue; item = q.popleft() # dequeue; is_empty = len(q)==0.

examples.input: Operations: enqueue(1), enqueue(2), dequeue(), enqueue(3), dequeue(), dequeue()
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[]. Step 2: enqueue(1) -> Queue=[1]. Step 3: enqueue(2) -> Queue=[1,2]. Step 4: dequeue() -> returns 1, Queue=[2]. Step 5: enqueue(3) -> Queue=[2,3]. Step 6: dequeue() -> returns 2, Queue=[3]. Step 7: dequeue() -> returns 3, Queue=[].
Step 1: Queue=[]. Step 2: enqueue(5) -> Queue=[5]. Step 3: dequeue() -> returns 5, Queue=[]. Step 4: enqueue(6) -> Queue=[6]. Step 5: dequeue() -> returns 6, Queue=[].

testCases.input: Operations: enqueue(1), enqueue(2), enqueue(3), dequeue(), dequeue()
Operations: enqueue(5), enqueue(6), dequeue(), enqueue(7), dequeue()
Operations: enqueue(0), dequeue(), enqueue(1), dequeue(), enqueue(2)
Operations: enqueue(8), enqueue(9), dequeue(), dequeue(), dequeue()
Operations: enqueue(100), dequeue(), enqueue(200), dequeue(), enqueue(300)
Operations: enqueue(1), enqueue(2), enqueue(3), dequeue(), enqueue(4), dequeue(), dequeue()

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs (nodes & edges) and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
Graphs consist of nodes (vertices) and edges (connections). We represent them using adjacency lists (dict or list of lists). 
Arrays are used to track visited nodes or parent pointers. This foundation supports all path-finding algorithms.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Identify edges: connection between 0 and 1, connection between 1 and 2. Step 3: Represent as pairs: (0,1) and (1,2).

examples.input: Graph with nodes: A,B,C; edges: A-B, B-C, C-A
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: A, B, C. Step 2: Identify edges: A connected to B, B connected to C, C connected to A. Step 3: Represent as pairs: (A,B), (B,C), (C,A).

testCases.input: Nodes: A,B,C,D; Edges: A-B, B-C, C-D
Nodes: M,N,O,P; Edges: M-N, N-O, O-P

approaches.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set) O(E α(V))
approaches.summary: Preprocess the graph by unioning connected nodes using a disjoint-set data structure, then check if the start and target nodes are in the same set. This approach is efficient for multiple queries on a static graph.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution to check if a path exists between two nodes in an undirected graph using Union-Find.
ladders.explanation: The Union-Find approach solves the path existence problem by treating it as a connectivity query. Instead of performing a search for each query, we preprocess the graph to group connected nodes into disjoint sets. The intuition is that two nodes are connected if and only if they belong to the same set after processing all edges. This is efficient for multiple queries on a static graph because the preprocessing is done once, and each query becomes a constant-time operation (amortized). The algorithm works by initializing each node as its own parent (representative), then iterating through all edges and unioning the sets of the two endpoint nodes. After preprocessing, checking if a path exists between nodes u and v reduces to checking if find(u) equals find(v). The time complexity is O(E α(V)) for preprocessing, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and O(α(V)) per query. Space complexity is O(V) for parent and rank arrays. Common mistakes include not handling both union-by-rank and path compression, which are essential for optimal performance, or incorrectly assuming the graph is directed (Union-Find works for undirected graphs). This connects to L1 by relying on the union operation to build connected sets.

examples.trace:
Edges [[0,1],[1,2]]: Union 0-1, then 1-2 → nodes 0,1,2 in same set.
Query (0,2): find(0) == find(2) → True (path exists).

Edges [[0,1],[2,3]]: Two separate components.
Query (0,3): find(0) != find(3) → False (no path).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start Node
ladders.desc: Implement the union operation to merge two disjoint sets, optimizing with union-by-rank.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is the core of building connected components in Union-Find. It takes two elements (nodes) and merges their sets into one. The intuition is that by unioning sets represented by their roots, we efficiently combine connected components. Union-by-rank is an optimization that attaches the shorter tree (by rank) under the root of the taller tree to keep the tree flat, minimizing the height for faster find operations. The algorithm first finds the roots of both elements using the find operation (with path compression, but that's handled separately). If the roots are different, we compare their ranks: if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY], set parent[rootX] = rootY; if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY], set parent[rootY] = rootX; if equal, choose one as parent and increment its rank by 1. Time complexity is O(α(V)) amortized due to find operations and constant-time union steps. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. Common mistakes include not using ranks correctly (e.g., always attaching arbitrarily, which can lead to tall trees), or unioning without finding roots first. This connects to L0 as it's used repeatedly to process all edges and build the connectivity structure.

examples.trace: Step 1: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Step 2: Roots are different (0 and 1). Step 3: Compare ranks: rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, they are equal. Step 4: Choose 1 as parent: set parent[0]=1. Step 5: Increment rank[1] to 1.
Step 1: find(1)=1, find(2)=2. Step 2: Roots are different (1 and 2). Step 3: Compare ranks: rank[1]=1, rank[2]=0, rank[1] > rank[2]. Step 4: Set parent[2]=1. No rank change.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Visited Tracking / Queue / Union
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation to locate the root of an element, optimizing with path compression.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is essential in Union-Find to determine the representative (root) of a set for an element. The intuition is that by recursively finding the parent until reaching the root, we can identify which set an element belongs to. Path compression optimizes this by flattening the tree during the find operation: as we traverse up to the root, we set the parent of each visited node directly to the root, reducing the height for future operations. This makes subsequent finds nearly constant time. The algorithm is recursive or iterative: if parent[x] != x, set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) and return parent[x]; otherwise, return x. Time complexity is O(α(V)) amortized due to path compression. Space complexity is O(1) extra space, but recursion uses O(log V) stack space in worst case (iterative version avoids this). Common mistakes include not implementing path compression (leading to slower finds), or using an iterative method without updating parents correctly. This connects to L1 as find is called within union to get roots, and to L0 for query checks.

examples.trace: Step 1: parent[0]=1, which is not 0. Step 2: Recursively find(1): parent[1]=1, so root is 1. Step 3: Set parent[0]=1 (path compression). Step 4: Return 1.
Step 1: parent[0]=1, not 0. Step 2: find(1): parent[1]=2, not 1. Step 3: find(2): parent[2]=2, root is 2. Step 4: Set parent[1]=2, then parent[0]=2. Return 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Represent disjoint sets using a parent array where each node points to its parent, with roots pointing to themselves.
ladders.explanation: The disjoint set data structure is represented as a forest of trees, where each tree corresponds to a set. The intuition is that each element has a parent pointer, and the root of the tree (which points to itself) serves as the representative of the set. This representation allows efficient union and find operations. We use an array parent of size V, where parent[i] stores the parent of node i. Initially, each node is its own parent: parent[i] = i, forming V singleton sets. This setup is crucial because it defines the starting state for connectivity. Time complexity for initialization is O(V), and space complexity is O(V). Common mistakes include not initializing parent array correctly (e.g., with -1 or other values), or misunderstanding that the array indices must correspond to node labels. This connects to L2 as the parent array is used in find operations, and to L1 for union updates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Create array parent of size 3. Step 2: For i from 0 to 2, set parent[i] = i. Result: parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2.
Step 1: Create array parent of size 5. Step 2: For i from 0 to 4, set parent[i] = i. Result: parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3, parent[4]=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Access / Queue Ops
ladders.desc: Initialize an array and access elements by index, fundamental for representing data structures.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are a basic data structure used to store elements in contiguous memory locations, allowing fast access by index. The intuition is that by using an array, we can represent relationships (like parent pointers) efficiently. Initialization involves creating an array of a given size and setting each element to a specific value (e.g., its index). Indexing allows retrieving or updating the value at a position. This operation is foundational because the parent array in Union-Find relies on correct initialization and indexing to map nodes to their parents. For example, to initialize parent array for V nodes, we create an array of size V and set parent[i] = i for all i. Time complexity is O(V) for initialization and O(1) for indexing. Space complexity is O(V). Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, incorrect array sizes, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. This connects to L3 as it's used to set up the parent array representation.

examples.input: Initialize array of size 3 with values equal to index
examples.trace: Step 1: Create empty array arr. Step 2: For i from 0 to 2, append i to arr. Result: arr[0]=0, arr[1]=1, arr[2]=2.
Step 1: Array arr has indices 0,1,2. Step 2: arr[1] refers to the element at index 1, which is 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs (nodes & edges) and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
Graphs consist of nodes (vertices) and edges (connections). We represent them using adjacency lists (dict or list of lists). 
Arrays are used to track visited nodes or parent pointers. This foundation supports all path-finding algorithms.

examples.output: Graph with 3 nodes and 2 edges connecting 0-1 and 1-2
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edge [0,1] connects node 0 and node 1. Step 3: Edge [1,2] connects node 1 and node 2. Step 4: Visualize or list connections: 0 is connected to 1, 1 is connected to 0 and 2, 2 is connected to 1.

examples.output: Graph with 3 nodes and 3 edges forming a triangle
examples.trace: Step 1: Nodes: A, B, C. Step 2: Edge [A,B] connects A and B. Step 3: Edge [B,C] connects B and C. Step 4: Edge [C,A] connects C and A. Step 5: All nodes are connected to each other.
